name: ISO/IEC 27001:2013
description: International standard for information security management systems (ISMS). Predecessor to ISO/IEC 27001:2022, providing requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an ISMS with 114 Annex A controls.
businessImpact: Organisations that achieve ISO/IEC 27001:2013 certification demonstrate to customers, partners and regulators that they have a systematic, risk-driven approach to protecting information assets. Certification is often a prerequisite for public-sector contracts and for operating in regulated industries. The standard requires documented policies, defined roles, regular internal audits and management reviews, which creates measurable overhead but also drives repeatable security processes. Non-conformities discovered during surveillance audits may result in suspension or withdrawal of certification, making continual improvement a business necessity. Transition to the 2022 edition was required by October 2025, imposing a re-certification cost on all certified organisations.
structure: The standard is divided into ten mandatory clauses and one normative annex. Clauses 1–3 cover scope, normative references and terms. Clauses 4–10 form the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle: Clause 4 (Context of the Organisation) defines internal and external issues and interested parties; Clause 5 (Leadership) sets management commitment and the information security policy; Clause 6 (Planning) covers risk assessment methodology and the Statement of Applicability; Clause 7 (Support) addresses resources, competence, awareness and documentation; Clause 8 (Operation) governs day-to-day execution of the risk treatment plan; Clause 9 (Performance Evaluation) mandates monitoring, measurement, internal audit and management review; Clause 10 (Improvement) requires nonconformity handling and continual improvement. Annex A lists 114 controls organised into 14 control categories (A.5–A.18), ranging from information security policies to supplier relationships and compliance.

name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022
description: International standard for information security management systems (ISMS). Provides requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an ISMS.
businessImpact: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 is the globally recognised benchmark for information security management. Achieving certification signals trustworthiness to clients, investors and regulators, and is increasingly required in procurement processes across sectors including finance, healthcare, cloud services and critical infrastructure. The 2022 revision introduced 11 new controls covering areas such as threat intelligence, cloud security and data masking, reflecting the modern threat landscape. Organisations certified under the 2013 edition had until 31 October 2025 to transition, requiring gap analysis, updated documentation, staff retraining and a recertification audit. The ongoing commitment includes annual surveillance audits and a full recertification cycle every three years, embedding security governance into the business operating model.
structure: The standard retains the ten-clause high-level structure aligned with other ISO management system standards (Annex SL / ISO Harmonized Structure). Clauses 4–10 define ISMS requirements covering context, leadership, planning, support, operation, performance evaluation and improvement. Annex A has been restructured from 114 controls in 14 categories to 93 controls organised into four themes: Organisational controls (37), People controls (8), Physical controls (14) and Technological controls (34). Each control is tagged with five attribute types (control type, information security properties, cybersecurity concepts, operational capabilities and security domains) to facilitate mapping against other frameworks. A companion standard, ISO/IEC 27002:2022, provides detailed guidance on implementing each Annex A control.

name: NIS2 Directive
description: EU Directive on measures for a high common level of cybersecurity across the Union (2022/2555). Replaces the original NIS Directive and significantly expands its scope.
businessImpact: NIS2 dramatically widens the pool of organisations subject to EU cybersecurity obligations. It covers entities in 18 sectors of high criticality (energy, transport, banking, health, digital infrastructure and others) and important sectors (postal services, waste management, manufacturing and more), applying to medium and large organisations by default and some smaller ones where they are critical. Organisations are classified as 'essential' or 'important', with stricter supervisory and enforcement regimes for essential entities. Management bodies are personally accountable for cybersecurity risk management, and executives can face temporary bans from management roles for repeated serious violations. Fines reach €10 million or 2% of global annual turnover for important entities, and €20 million or 4% for essential entities. Member states had until 17 October 2024 to transpose the directive into national law, creating a patchwork of national implementations that businesses operating across borders must track.
structure: The Directive consists of 10 chapters and 46 articles. Chapter I (General provisions) defines scope, entities and key terms. Chapter II (Cybersecurity risk management and reporting obligations) is the operational core: Article 21 requires organisations to adopt appropriate technical, operational and organisational measures across ten minimum security areas including incident handling, supply chain security, vulnerability disclosure and basic cyber hygiene. Article 23 mandates incident reporting in three stages: early warning within 24 hours, notification within 72 hours, and a final report within one month. Chapter III (Cybersecurity information sharing) establishes voluntary and mandatory information-sharing arrangements. Chapters IV–V address cross-border jurisdiction, registration in national databases and EU-level coordination through NIS Cooperation Group, CSIRTs network and EU-CyCLONe. Chapter VI covers European cybersecurity certification. Chapter VII contains final provisions including the repeal of the original NIS Directive.

name: General Data Protection Regulation
description: EU regulation on data protection and privacy (2016/679). Sets rules for the processing of personal data and ensures the protection of fundamental rights of individuals.
businessImpact: GDPR applies to any organisation that processes personal data of individuals in the EU, regardless of where the organisation is established. This extraterritorial reach means global businesses must comply. Data controllers must implement appropriate technical and organisational security measures, appoint a Data Protection Officer in certain circumstances, maintain records of processing activities, perform Data Protection Impact Assessments for high-risk processing, and notify supervisory authorities of personal data breaches within 72 hours. Non-compliance carries fines of up to €20 million or 4% of global annual turnover, whichever is higher. Organisations relying on standard contractual clauses or other transfer mechanisms to export personal data outside the EEA face continuous scrutiny following the Schrems II ruling, requiring regular legal basis reviews.
structure: The GDPR comprises 11 chapters and 99 articles, accompanied by 173 recitals providing interpretive guidance. Chapter I (General provisions) sets out subject matter, material and territorial scope, and key definitions. Chapter II (Principles) establishes the six lawful processing bases and seven data protection principles. Chapter III (Rights of the data subject) confers rights including access, rectification, erasure, portability and objection. Chapter IV (Controller and processor) defines obligations for those who handle data, including contracts, records of processing activities, security, breach notification and DPIAs. Chapter V (Transfers of personal data to third countries) governs international data flows. Chapters VI–VII establish supervisory authorities and the European Data Protection Board. Chapters VIII–XI address remedies, liability, penalties and final provisions. Key accountability mechanisms include the one-stop-shop mechanism for cross-border cases and binding corporate rules for intra-group transfers.

name: Digital Operational Resilience Act
description: EU regulation on digital operational resilience for the financial sector (2022/2554). Harmonises ICT risk management requirements for financial entities across the EU.
businessImpact: DORA became applicable on 17 January 2025 and directly affects more than 22,000 financial entities operating in the EU, including banks, insurance companies, investment firms, crypto-asset service providers, payment institutions and critical ICT third-party service providers (CTPPs). Because it is a Regulation rather than a Directive it is directly applicable across all member states, eliminating regulatory fragmentation. Financial entities must establish a comprehensive ICT risk management framework, classify and report major ICT-related incidents to regulators within strict timeframes, conduct threat-led penetration testing (TLPT) every three years for significant entities, and rigorously manage third-party ICT risk through contractual provisions and ongoing oversight. CTPPs are subject to direct supervision by lead European Supervisory Authorities. Non-compliance with DORA can trigger supervisory measures including fines and, for CTPPs, suspension of services to financial entities.
structure: DORA is structured in 9 chapters and 64 articles supplemented by Level 2 regulatory technical standards (RTS) and implementing technical standards (ITS) developed by the European Supervisory Authorities (EBA, EIOPA, ESMA). Chapter I (General provisions) defines scope and terms. Chapter II (ICT risk management) requires financial entities to maintain a sound, comprehensive ICT risk management framework including strategy, policies, continuous monitoring, business continuity and disaster recovery. Chapter III (ICT-related incident management, classification and reporting) establishes harmonised incident classification criteria and three-phase reporting obligations to competent authorities. Chapter IV (Digital operational resilience testing) mandates basic testing for all entities and advanced TLPT for significant entities. Chapter V (Managing ICT third-party risk) requires contractual safeguards, exit strategies and concentration risk management. Chapter VI (Information-sharing arrangements) encourages voluntary sharing of cyber threat intelligence. Chapters VII–IX cover the CTPP oversight framework, transitional provisions and final provisions.

name: CIS Controls v8
description: Prioritised set of actions to protect organisations and data from known cyber attack vectors. Developed by the Center for Internet Security.
businessImpact: The CIS Controls are a practical, prioritised set of security actions designed to stop today's most pervasive and dangerous attacks. Version 8 was reorganised around asset types rather than organisational roles, reflecting cloud and mobile-first environments. The three Implementation Groups (IG1, IG2, IG3) allow organisations of all sizes to adopt the Controls proportionally: IG1 represents essential cyber hygiene suitable for small organisations with limited security expertise; IG2 adds controls for organisations handling sensitive data; IG3 covers all 153 Safeguards for organisations facing sophisticated threats. Because the Controls are voluntary, their direct legal weight is limited, but regulators, cyber insurers and procurement processes increasingly reference them. Adopting the CIS Controls supports compliance with multiple mandatory frameworks (NIST CSF, ISO 27001, HIPAA, PCI DSS) through built-in crosswalks, reducing duplicate effort.
structure: CIS Controls v8 contains 18 Controls and 153 Safeguards (previously called Sub-Controls). The Controls are grouped around asset types: CIS Control 1 (Inventory and Control of Enterprise Assets) and 2 (Inventory and Control of Software Assets) establish asset visibility; Controls 3–4 cover data protection and secure configuration; Controls 5–6 address account management and access control; Controls 7–10 deal with vulnerability management, audit log management, email and web browser protections; Controls 11–16 cover network monitoring, malware defences, data recovery, network infrastructure management, network monitoring and defence, and application software security; Controls 17–18 address incident response and penetration testing. Each Safeguard specifies an asset type, security function and Implementation Group assignment, providing clear prioritisation. Comprehensive crosswalk spreadsheets map Safeguards to NIST CSF, ISO 27001, CMMC, HIPAA and other frameworks.

name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
description: Voluntary framework for managing and reducing cybersecurity risk, organised around six core functions: Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover.
businessImpact: Although voluntary for most private sector organisations, NIST CSF 2.0 has become the de facto reference architecture for cybersecurity programmes globally. US federal agencies are effectively required to align with it, and it is widely referenced in cyber insurance underwriting, M&A due diligence, contractual security requirements and regulatory guidance from sectors including energy, financial services and healthcare. Version 2.0 (released February 2024) broadened the intended audience from critical infrastructure to all organisations and introduced the Govern function to emphasise cybersecurity governance, strategy and supply-chain risk as executive-level responsibilities. Organisations use the framework's tiers (Partial, Risk Informed, Repeatable, Adaptive) to benchmark maturity, and the profile mechanism to document current and target states, enabling gap analysis and resource prioritisation without prescribing a one-size-fits-all control catalogue.
structure: NIST CSF 2.0 is organised around six Core Functions that represent high-level cybersecurity outcomes: Govern (GV) — establish and monitor cybersecurity risk strategy, policy, roles and supply-chain risk management; Identify (ID) — understand organisational context, assets and risks; Protect (PR) — implement safeguards to manage cybersecurity risks; Detect (DE) — discover and analyse cybersecurity events; Respond (RS) — take action on detected incidents; Recover (RC) — restore capabilities impaired by incidents. Each function is subdivided into Categories (22 total) and Subcategories (106 total), each representing a specific, measurable outcome. The framework also defines Tiers (1–4) describing the sophistication of risk governance, and Profiles (Current and Target) that organisations use to communicate their cybersecurity posture and prioritise investments. NIST publishes companion resources including Quick Start Guides, informative references and implementation examples.

name: KRITIS / IT-Sicherheitsgesetz 2.0
description: German regulation for operators of critical infrastructure (Kritische Infrastrukturen) under the BSI Act (BSIG) and IT Security Act 2.0 (IT-Sicherheitsgesetz 2.0, 2021). Mandates state-of-the-art technical and organisational security measures, attack detection systems, proof of compliance every two years, and incident reporting obligations for critical infrastructure operators.
businessImpact: The IT-Sicherheitsgesetz 2.0 (IT-SiG 2.0), enacted in May 2021, significantly extended the obligations of operators of critical infrastructure in Germany. Organisations in sectors such as energy, water, transport, healthcare, finance and digital infrastructure that meet sector-specific threshold values are classified as KRITIS operators. They must implement 'state of the art' technical and organisational security measures for their critical components, deploy systems for attack detection (§ 8a BSIG), register with and report significant incidents to the Federal Office for Information Security (BSI) within 24 hours, and provide proof of compliance to the BSI every two years through qualified audits, certifications or self-assessments. The 2.0 amendment introduced a new category of 'organisations of special public interest' (UNBÖFI) with lighter but real obligations, broadening the regulated perimeter. Non-compliance can result in administrative fines of up to €20 million, BSI enforcement notices and potential exclusion of untrusted ICT components from critical systems.
structure: The KRITIS regime derives from the BSI Act (Gesetz über das Bundesamt für Sicherheit in der Informationstechnik, BSIG) as amended by the IT Security Act 2.0 (BGBl. I 2021, Nr. 25). The key operative provisions are: § 2 BSIG (definitions, including 'critical infrastructure' by sector and threshold value); § 8a BSIG (security measures obligation and biennial proof of compliance); § 8b BSIG (point of contact registration and 24-hour incident reporting); § 8c BSIG (obligations for digital service providers under the original NIS Directive); § 8d BSIG (obligations for organisations of special public interest). Sector-specific threshold values are defined in the KRITIS Regulation (BSI-KritisV), which lists operators in nine sectors (energy, water, food, information technology, transport, health, finance/insurance, media/culture, municipal waste). The BSI publishes an Orientierungshilfe (guidance document) specifying the minimum measures expected under § 8a.

name: NIS2-Umsetzungsgesetz (NIS2UmsuCG)
description: German national implementation of the EU NIS2 Directive (2022/2555). The NIS-2-Umsetzungs- und Cybersicherheitsstärkungsgesetz transposes the NIS2 requirements into German law, extends and replaces the existing IT Security Act framework, and introduces strengthened obligations for essential and important entities including management accountability and supply chain security.
businessImpact: The NIS2UmsuCG is Germany's transposition of the EU NIS2 Directive and is expected to affect around 29,000 organisations, a dramatic increase from the roughly 1,600 entities covered by the previous KRITIS regime. Both essential and important entities must register with the BSI, implement a comprehensive set of cybersecurity risk management measures (largely aligned with NIS2 Article 21), report significant incidents within 24 hours (initial notification) and 72 hours (detailed notification), and manage supply-chain security. A standout feature is personal management liability: executives can be held personally liable for failing to fulfil cybersecurity oversight duties, and competent authorities may temporarily prohibit natural persons in management positions from exercising their responsibilities at repeated violations. Fines for essential entities reach €10 million or 2% of global annual turnover; for important entities €7 million or 1.4% of global annual turnover. The law also replaces and modernises the existing BSIG and IT-SiG 2.0 framework, creating a unified legal basis for cybersecurity regulation in Germany.
structure: The NIS2UmsuCG amends multiple pieces of German legislation, but its core is a new version of the BSI Act (BSIG). The key structural elements include: definitions of essential and important entities by sector and size criteria (§ 28 BSIG-E); a comprehensive list of mandatory risk-management measures covering at minimum ten areas including incident handling, business continuity, supply-chain security, vulnerability disclosure and cryptography (§ 30 BSIG-E, mirroring NIS2 Art. 21); tiered incident reporting obligations to the BSI (§ 32 BSIG-E); registration requirements and designation of a point of contact (§ 33 BSIG-E); enforcement powers of the BSI including audits and sanctions (§§ 60–65 BSIG-E); and management body liability provisions (§ 38 BSIG-E). The law also amends the EnWG, TKG and other sector-specific laws to align sectoral regulators with the new cybersecurity framework, and transposes the EU CER Directive on physical resilience of critical entities.

name: BSI IT-Grundschutz
description: Comprehensive information security methodology published by Germany's Federal Office for Information Security (BSI). Consists of BSI Standards (200-1 through 200-4) and the IT-Grundschutz Compendium, providing building blocks for establishing and operating an ISMS. Widely used by German public authorities and recognised as equivalent to ISO/IEC 27001.
businessImpact: BSI IT-Grundschutz is the dominant information security methodology in German public administration and is widely adopted by companies supplying the German public sector, regulated utilities and large enterprises. The IT-Grundschutz certification (awarded by BSI-licensed auditors) is accepted as equivalent to ISO/IEC 27001 and fulfils the two-year compliance proof requirement under KRITIS (§ 8a BSIG). German federal and state authorities are largely obliged to follow the methodology; private organisations adopt it to simplify compliance with German and EU regulatory requirements. The modular 'Grundschutz' approach provides pre-defined standard safeguards for typical IT components, which reduces the cost of creating a bespoke risk treatment plan. The BSI updates the Compendium annually, adding new building blocks for emerging technologies (cloud, IoT, industrial control systems), requiring organisations to review applicability of new modules annually and adjust their security documentation accordingly.
structure: The methodology rests on four BSI Standards and the IT-Grundschutz Compendium. BSI Standard 200-1 (ISMS) aligns with ISO/IEC 27001 and defines the requirements for an information security management system. BSI Standard 200-2 (IT-Grundschutz Methodology) describes three implementation approaches: Basic Protection (Basis-Absicherung) for quick entry-level protection; Standard Protection (Standard-Absicherung), the primary approach equivalent to ISO 27001; and Core Protection (Kern-Absicherung) for a focused asset-level approach. BSI Standard 200-3 (Risk Analysis) provides a streamlined risk method complementing Standard Protection. BSI Standard 200-4 (Business Continuity Management) aligns with ISO 22301. The IT-Grundschutz Compendium (updated annually) contains building blocks (Bausteine) organised into ten process layers (ISMS, ORP, CON, OPS, DER, APP, SYS, IND, NET, INF) and an overarching ISMS layer. Each building block specifies elementary threats, requirements at three levels (Basic, Standard, High Protection) and cross-references to ISO 27001 controls and other standards.
